original_abstract: Time is our most precious, nonrenewable resource, yet its mismanagement remains a critical barrier to addressing the global crises of climate change, economic inequality, and regional insecurity. While often overlooked, the squandering of time—not only our own but that of nature and others—destabilizes efforts to prevent violence and build sustainable peace. This article argues that we must radically overhaul our relationship with temporality to ensure collective well-being. By defining the nature of time and surveying current temporal deficiencies, the study proposes a framework for a more adaptive and responsible "temporament" specifically tailored to the field of conflict transformation.
humanized_abstract: Time is a unique and finite resource. And so, it is wasted all around us. Wasted in so many areas: environment, economy and society. But not least, it is wasted for other people and living beings – as well as nature itself. Consequently, the wasting of our time, but also the time of others, becomes a decisive obstacle to preventing crises like climate change or to preventing violent conflicts in various parts of the world. We no longer look at the wasting of our time and that of others in this context – yet our way of dealing with time and time structures, which we call temporament, also plays a decisive role in conflict prevention and the preservation of lasting peace. In order to fundamentally secure the future of humanity, society and nature, a new temporament and corresponding time forms must be developed from scratch in the context of peacework. To this end, we first of all need to establish the time conception and the necessary characteristics of the temporal conditions that we encounter in everyday life and in our professional environment and that are – without exception – incompatible with the desired time forms for peacework.
